title: Does dishwasher eco mode actually save money?
description: Dishwasher eco mode cuts running cost by around a third by heating less water to a lower temperature. Here is the real saving in pounds at UK 2026 rates.
blurb: Eco mode runs longer but heats less water, cutting a dishwasher's running cost by roughly a third.
lede: Eco mode is the cheapest cycle on almost every dishwasher sold in the UK, yet it is also the one people skip most often because it takes two or three hours to finish. That long run time is exactly why it saves money. The machine is heating a smaller amount of water to a lower temperature and then circulating it for longer, instead of blasting your plates with very hot water in a hurry. Heating water is where nearly all the electricity goes, so a cooler, slower wash genuinely costs less to run. Below are the real numbers at the July 2026 price cap so you can see what eco mode is worth on your own bill.
short_answer: Yes. A typical eco cycle uses around 0.8 kWh against roughly 1.3 to 1.4 kWh for a standard or quick wash, because it heats less water to a lower temperature. At 26.11p per kWh that is about 21p a cycle versus 35p, a saving near 14p each time. Run the dishwasher five times a week and eco mode saves roughly £36 a year.

sections.h2: Why eco mode costs less even though it runs longer
sections.html:
The thing that makes a dishwasher expensive to run is not the motor that sprays the water around. It is the heating element. Warming the water up accounts for the large majority of a cycle's electricity use, so the temperature the machine chooses matters far more than how long it runs.

A standard or quick programme heats water to around 60 to 65C and gets the job done fast. Eco mode heats to roughly 45 to 50C instead, then makes up for the gentler temperature by recirculating that water for longer and giving the detergent more time to work. Less heat plus a longer soak gets dishes just as clean while drawing noticeably less power.

So the long run time is the saving, not a hidden cost. The element spends most of the cycle switched off while the pump quietly does the work. This is the same principle behind washing clothes at 30 degrees: lower the water temperature and the bill follows it down.

sections.h2: What eco mode actually costs at 2026 rates
sections.html:
Figures vary by model, but most full-size dishwashers land close to the numbers below. The standard and quick programmes cluster around 1.3 to 1.4 kWh per cycle; eco sits near 0.8 kWh.

CycleEnergy per washCost per washCost per year (5 washes a week)
Eco0.8 kWh21p£54
Standard / auto1.35 kWh35p£92
Quick wash1.3 kWh34p£88

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Energy per cycle is a typical mid-range figure; check your own model's label for exact values.

The headline is the gap between the top and bottom rows. Choosing eco over the standard programme saves roughly 14p a wash, which is about £38 a year for a household running the machine five times a week, and more if you run it daily. Quick wash feels frugal because it is fast, but it heats water hard to cut corners on time, so it costs almost as much as the full standard cycle.

sections.h2: When eco mode is worth it and when it is not
sections.html:
Eco mode is the right default for the overnight or daytime wash that nobody is waiting on. Load it, press start, walk away. The two to three hour run time is irrelevant if you are asleep or out.

It is the wrong choice in two situations. First, when you genuinely need the dishes back fast, for example mid dinner party, the quick wash earns its higher cost by saving you time. Second, eco programmes wash cooler, so very greasy roasting tins or baked-on food can come out needing a second go. A wasted eco cycle plus a rewash costs more than getting it right first time on a hotter programme. For everyday plates, glasses and cutlery, eco handles it without trouble.

One habit beats the cycle choice entirely: only run a full load. A half-empty dishwasher on any programme wastes water and electricity. Scrape rather than pre-rinse under a hot tap, since pre-rinsing can use more hot water than the machine itself. There is more on getting the most from the appliance in our guide to dishwasher efficiency.

sections.h2: Does the long run time push up standby cost?
sections.html:
A common worry is that a three hour cycle leaves the machine drawing power for hours, cancelling out the saving. It does not. The pump and electronics use a tiny fraction of what the heating element uses, so a long low-temperature cycle still totals less energy than a short hot one. The kWh figure already includes the whole run, start to finish.

The genuinely wasteful habit is leaving the dishwasher switched on at the wall after it finishes, where the display and electronics trickle a small amount of power around the clock. It is minor on a single appliance but adds up across a kitchen full of them. Our guide to standby power covers how to find and stop those background draws, and a plug-in energy monitor lets you measure your own machine's eco cycle exactly rather than trusting the average.

sections.h2: How eco mode fits the energy label
sections.html:
The energy rating printed on a new dishwasher is measured on the eco programme, not the standard one. That is why a machine rated A or B can still cost more than you expect if you only ever use the quick wash. The label is showing you the appliance at its most efficient, and eco mode is how you actually reach that figure.

If you are choosing a new machine, the label's annual energy use in kWh is the number to compare, and a more efficient model widens the gap eco mode already gives you. Our guide to energy labels explains how to read the rating and the kWh-per-100-cycles figure, and the appliance running cost hub puts a dishwasher alongside the other kitchen machines so you can see where your money really goes.

Eco mode saves money, and the saving is real rather than marketing. By heating less water to a lower temperature, a typical eco cycle costs around 21p against roughly 35p for the standard or quick wash at the July 2026 price cap. Across a year of normal use that is somewhere between £35 and £45 off your bill, for the price of pressing a different button and accepting a longer run time.

Make eco your default, only ever run a full load, skip the hot pre-rinse, and keep the quick wash for the rare times you actually need the dishes back in an hour. Do that and the dishwasher quietly becomes one of the cheapest appliances in the kitchen to run.

faq.q: How much does dishwasher eco mode save per year?
faq.a: For a household running the machine five times a week, eco mode saves roughly £35 to £45 a year against the standard or quick wash. The saving is about 14p per cycle at 26.11p per kWh, the Ofgem cap for July to September 2026, and it grows if you run the dishwasher daily.

faq.q: Why does eco mode take so long if it saves energy?
faq.a: It heats water to a lower temperature, around 45 to 50C instead of 60 to 65C, then circulates that cooler water for longer to get the same cleaning result. Heating water uses most of a dishwasher's electricity, so a longer cooler wash still uses less power overall than a short hot one.

faq.q: Is quick wash cheaper than eco mode?
faq.a: No. Quick wash heats water hard to save time, so it uses almost as much energy as the full standard cycle, around 1.3 kWh against eco's 0.8 kWh. Quick wash is worth its higher cost only when you genuinely need the dishes back fast.

faq.q: Does eco mode clean as well as a normal cycle?
faq.a: For everyday plates, glasses and cutlery, yes. Because it washes cooler, very greasy roasting tins or heavily baked-on food can occasionally need a second go, so use a hotter programme for those. Running only full loads and skipping the hot pre-rinse helps eco mode perform at its best.

faq.q: Does leaving the dishwasher on standby cancel out the saving?
faq.a: No. The long eco run time uses very little power once the water is heated, and the kWh figure already covers the whole cycle. The small ongoing waste comes from leaving the machine switched on at the wall afterwards, which trickles a little standby power around the clock.
